utterances: And Natalie Weiner is a staff writer for the Bleacher Report, the digital sports news outlet.

Good to have you with us as well, Natalie.

So the NFL playoffs start today.
So we decided to keep things NFL focus.
And, no, we're not talking scores.
We want to talk about some of the big issues in and around the sport, which is - like it or not - the biggest ratings driver on television.
So, you know, in that sense, it's an important, you know, cultural institution.
And I want to start with an issue that goes beyond football that was in the news this week.

And that is the Trump administration's decision to reverse Obama administration's guidance to federal prosecutors which was to shield legal marijuana from federal involvement expect - except in narrow circumstances, such as cartel activity or where it's suspected to being sold to minors.
And so we're going to have more on that in this program later.
But I'm bringing this up because the issue of marijuana has been a big one in pro sports, including the NFL.
So Kevin Blackistone, I'll start with you.
What's the state of play on this?
No. No.
To give us their take, we've got education analyst and blogger Casey Lartigue.
His blog is ""The Casey Lartigue Show."" Also, Jozen Cummings.
He writes the blog ""Broke Thoughts"" King magazine's Web site.
He is also the site's editor.
And Mark Anthony Neal, he's a professor of African and African-American studies at Duke University.
He also writes the blog, ""Critical Noir,"" for Vive magazine online.
Welcome, everybody.
Hey, Farai.
Hey.
Hey, Happy New Year.
Happy New Year.
So Mark, I'm going to start with you.
Denzel Washington's latest movie, ""The Great Debaters"" is stirring up some controversy about how much it actually sticks to the historically record.
Last month, we talked with Washington about the film and he told us he thinks films and social issues interact.
I think some if it reflects society.
Sometimes, they lead the trends, but I think basically they reflect what's going on in the world and what people are talking about and what they enjoy.
Films are also escape.
I remember doing a film years ago, ""Cry Freedom"" that I thought was a very serious movie.
And I felt very serious about it.
And all -people, everybody needs to see it.
And a buddy of mine, who's a doctor, who deals with life and death issues all day long, he's like, Denzel, you know, I love you, but all day long I deal with life and death.
When I go to the movies, all I want to do is eat some popcorn and forget.
So there's a question over how much people want to see a movie like this that deals with racial uplift.
Then, there's the question of why the film changed the setting of the great debate from Southern California to U.S.C.
campus to Harvard.
And then, there, among other things, a scene where Denzel Washington has the debate team's coach Mel Tolson, tells the story behind lynching, the Willy Lynch story.
So Mark, why don't you unpack the Willy Lynch story for us a little bit.
And is it important if it's true?
I think the critical thing about the Willy Lynch story is that, you know, it's not about whether Willy Lynch existed.
You know, for all intents and purposes, many historians have weighed in on the fact that the Willy Lynch story is pretty much a myth or a hoax.
I mean, Williams Jelani Cobb, you know, wrote a few years ago for Africana.com, you know, being very explicit about this reality.

It doesn't mean that some of the logic that underlies what Willy Lynch represents, the idea of dividing folks and creating jealousies, and, you know, being violent against certain figures in a very public way so that it feeds down to other folks who were enslaved.
I mean, the logic behind it, I think, is very useful to put out into the story.

And I think, you know, if they were trying to really make a point that could stick educationally in the context of this film, it would have been helpful for Mel Tolson's - Denzel Washington's character, Mel Tolson, to really discuss that story within the context of the logic as opposed to just kind of signing off on something that many folks are very clear now as basically a hoax.
So, you have this story, Willy Lynch, who, at least, in the story, it's the inventor of lynching.
All right.
And it's — you're arguing, if I get this right, that it's an important parable, but it's just not true.
It's not just true.
Right.
And again, it's not unusual in the context of this film because this film does take great license with the truth throughout.
You know, everything from, as you've just mentioned, you know, where the actual great debate occurred.
The fact, that you know, two of the three prominent characters, in fact, composite figures.

And in fact, those composite figures, you know, take up so much space on the screen that James Farmer Jr., who's actually, you know, the real historical figure here, really, you know, takes a back seat to them.

And his narrative is critically important because it's, you know, those lessons he learned in the debate team that translate into his involvement with the founding of core and literally had an important political legacy that went on, you know, until his death.
And we got no inkling of that in the film because so much of the film was about, you know, the feel-good narrative of the year dynamic of it.
You know, let's watch the little engine could beat great, big Harvard, you know, in a debate.
Yeah.
James Farmer Jr. founding the Congress of Racial Equality.
But sorry, go ahead, jump in.
Yeah.
This is Casey.
I want to say I think it's actually tragic that they used the Willy Lynch letter which - or speech.
I mean, it's a piece of fiction.
There's no need to exaggerate bad things that have gone on in black history.
There's no need to bring in something that's clearly an urban legend when you're doing a story that is based on at least some truth.
So, actually, I think it was a mistake that they did.
I mean, there are so many of these urban legends out there that make people paranoid.
And maybe people are, you know, should be paranoid, but why bring in some fiction?

What about the whole idea that you can mix these composite characters with a real character like James Farmer Jr.? Is it okay to put characters that are like historical characters, on par with characters who actually lived and breathed?
I think it's acceptable.
You should be allowed to have some creative license.
People shouldn't use Hollywood and movies as their history teacher.
If you're letting these movies like - if you're letting these movies teach you something and you take their word as gold, then you have a real problem.
They shouldn't be your primary source of knowledge.
A movie like ""The Great Debaters"" should peek your curiosity, not necessarily satisfy it.
So...
Yeah.
I understand that I ought to be there, but...
So yeah, it's not a bad idea to kind of mix these things in.
But I start to worry when it's like, well, you know, the truth in that stuff is okay, we can just kind of bring these things in.
Actually, I say it's a mistake to bring this particular thing in.
Jozen, what about the whole idea of what people want to see, black, white or purple people.
You know, you have this movie, ""Great Debaters,"" making only a quarter of the amount per screen in its opening weekend as ""I Am Legend,"" the Will Smith zombie movie.
Mm-hmm.

That basically means, you know, regardless of how many theaters are playing in - and ""I Am Legend"" is playing in more - people really went out to see ""I Am Legend"" and they went out, but not as hard or not as fast to go see ""The Great Debaters.
Right.
Are people just more interested in escapism?
That's kind of what Denzel Washington was saying.
I think so.
I think that - I believe that there's such a thing as black Hollywood and the black film.
And I think ""The Great Debaters"" is such a film.
Most black films aren't made with delusions of grandeur.
Studios don't expect the black film to announce like world breaking box office numbers.
They expect the black film to be popular within its own communities.
So, I think when we're talking about the success of ""The Great Debaters,"" we need to look beyond the box office numbers.
I really do believe that Oprah and Denzel are happy with the fact that they were able to tell the story and release it.
It's a so-called passion project.
When Denzel did ""American Gangster,"" which did open at $43.6 million, opening weekend, he was appearing on the cover of ""Entertainment Weekly"" with Russell Crowe.
With ""The Great Debaters,"" he's appearing on the cover of ""Ebony"" with Oprah.
So, you look at something like that and you realize after then, this wasn't about making as much money as it possibly can.
Rather this was about being able to use their star power to get the kind of movie they want to see made.
So, you really - I mean, yes, I'm sure the studio (unintelligible) concern with the box office numbers and everything.
They really have to look beyond that as just being a definitive side of success.
Mark, what's taking…
But you know what, one of the…
Oh, go head.

Yeah, I just wanted to add, you know, (unintelligible) said, you know, it is a historical period film, which don't do well unless, you know, Leonardo DiCaprio is starring in it and I think, you know, the filmmakers tried to make up some concessions.

I mean, the relationship between Nate Parker's character and Samantha Booke, you know, that was the kind of character and the kind that focus on the sexual tension between them that they hope would bring young audiences to the film.

Mark, 14 members of the Congressional Black Caucus sent a letter to the Louisiana governor Kathleen Blanco, asking her to pardon the Jena Six.
Now, at the time that Jena Six was really raging publicly, particularly the Mychal Bell case, there was some criticism of the Congressional Black Caucus that they didn't ask for enough.
Is now the right time too late?
Is this still important to ask for a pardon?

I think for them, they got back to their hometowns, they got back to their constituents, and their constituents since called them on the fact that they weren't very vocal and aggressive enough around the Jena Six.
The rally, though, is that I think this really is an effort for them to do something very publicly.
The response to their constituents.

And while I think - you know, it's something that their passionate about and is something that they really feel powerfully about, the reality I fear is, so many other black political prisoners, many of which had been incarcerated because of things like COINTELPRO, the Counter Intelligence Program, the J.
Edgar Hoover's FBI, that really need to be dealt with.

And the fact that, you know, they're not really putting feet to the ground to deal with quote, end quote, ""legitimate political prisoners,"" and instead they're spending time on Jena Six in response to their constituents.
Right.
